source: McNeese & Hoag, Engineering and Technical Handbook, Tables 46-48
description: Complete trigonometric identity reference for engineering applications
note: These identities are the vocabulary of the unit circle — every identity is a statement about circular symmetry.

identities.name: Pythagorean identity (sine-cosine)
identities.proof_hint: From the unit circle: point (cosθ, sinθ) lies on x² + y² = 1.
identities.nasa_application: Normalization of direction cosines in attitude determination. Any rotation matrix row/column must satisfy this.

identities.name: Pythagorean identity (tangent-secant)
identities.proof_hint: Divide sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 by cos²θ.
identities.nasa_application: Simplification of radar range equations and antenna gain calculations.

identities.name: Pythagorean identity (cotangent-cosecant)
identities.proof_hint: Divide sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 by sin²θ.
identities.nasa_application: Appears in gravitational potential expansions using Legendre functions.

identities.name: Reciprocal identity (cosecant)
identities.proof_hint: Definition of cosecant as the reciprocal of sine.
identities.nasa_application: Signal amplitude normalization in radio telemetry when sine envelope is known.

identities.name: Reciprocal identity (secant)
identities.proof_hint: Definition of secant as the reciprocal of cosine.
identities.nasa_application: Appears in lens and mirror equations for telescope optics (Hubble, JWST).

identities.name: Reciprocal identity (cotangent)
identities.proof_hint: Definition of cotangent as the reciprocal of tangent.
identities.nasa_application: Trajectory deflection angle calculations in gravity assist maneuvers.

identities.name: Quotient identity (tangent)
identities.proof_hint: From unit circle: tanθ = y/x = sinθ/cosθ.
identities.nasa_application: Flight path angle computation: γ = arctan(v_r / v_t) in orbital mechanics.

identities.name: Quotient identity (cotangent)
identities.proof_hint: From unit circle: cotθ = x/y = cosθ/sinθ.
identities.nasa_application: Reciprocal of flight path angle tangent; appears in orbit equation rearrangements.

identities.name: Cofunction (sine-cosine)
identities.proof_hint: In a right triangle, the complement of one acute angle is the other. The side opposite one is adjacent to the other.
identities.nasa_application: Converting between elevation and zenith angles in antenna pointing and star tracker measurements.

identities.name: Cofunction (cosine-sine)
identities.proof_hint: Complement of the sine cofunction identity.
identities.nasa_application: Coordinate frame rotations between body-fixed and inertial frames.

identities.name: Cofunction (tangent-cotangent)
identities.proof_hint: Follows from sin/cos cofunction identities applied to the quotient.
identities.nasa_application: Switching between slope and reciprocal slope in terrain mapping (Mars rover path planning).

identities.name: Cofunction (cotangent-tangent)
identities.proof_hint: Reciprocal of the tangent cofunction identity.
identities.nasa_application: Complementary angle relationships in structural load analysis.

identities.name: Cofunction (secant-cosecant)
identities.proof_hint: sec = 1/cos, and cos(π/2 - θ) = sinθ, so sec(π/2 - θ) = 1/sinθ = cscθ.
identities.nasa_application: Reciprocal relationships in waveguide mode calculations.

identities.name: Cofunction (cosecant-secant)
identities.proof_hint: csc = 1/sin, and sin(π/2 - θ) = cosθ, so csc(π/2 - θ) = 1/cosθ = secθ.
identities.nasa_application: Complementary angle substitution in spherical trigonometry for celestial navigation.

identities.name: Odd function (sine)
identities.proof_hint: Reflecting (cosθ, sinθ) across the x-axis gives (cosθ, -sinθ). The angle -θ maps to this reflection.
identities.nasa_application: Symmetry exploitation in Fourier analysis of vibration data — odd harmonics in structural resonance.

identities.name: Even function (cosine)
identities.proof_hint: Reflecting across the x-axis preserves the x-coordinate (cosine).
identities.nasa_application: Antenna pattern symmetry — cosine patterns are symmetric about boresight, simplifying gain calculations.

identities.name: Odd function (tangent)
identities.proof_hint: tan(-θ) = sin(-θ)/cos(-θ) = -sinθ/cosθ = -tanθ.
identities.nasa_application: Sign conventions in control system feedback — negative angle produces negative torque command.

identities.name: Odd function (cotangent)
identities.proof_hint: cot(-θ) = cos(-θ)/sin(-θ) = cosθ/(-sinθ) = -cotθ.
identities.nasa_application: Antisymmetric terms in spherical harmonic gravity field expansions.

identities.name: Even function (secant)
identities.proof_hint: sec(-θ) = 1/cos(-θ) = 1/cosθ = secθ.
identities.nasa_application: Even symmetry in optical path length calculations for interferometry.

identities.name: Odd function (cosecant)
identities.proof_hint: csc(-θ) = 1/sin(-θ) = 1/(-sinθ) = -cscθ.
identities.nasa_application: Sign handling in numerical integration of orbital elements near singularities.

identities.name: Sine of sum
identities.proof_hint: Rotate point (1,0) by α then by β on the unit circle. Project onto y-axis.
identities.nasa_application: Combining rotation angles in sequential attitude maneuvers. Doppler shift calculations for moving sources.

identities.name: Sine of difference
identities.proof_hint: Replace β with -β in the sum formula and apply even/odd identities.
identities.nasa_application: Phase difference extraction in interferometric measurements (VLBI, gravity wave detection).

identities.name: Cosine of sum
identities.proof_hint: Rotate point (1,0) by α then by β on the unit circle. Project onto x-axis.
identities.nasa_application: Rotation matrix composition — the (1,1) element of R(α)R(β) is exactly this formula.

identities.name: Cosine of difference
identities.proof_hint: Replace β with -β in the sum formula and apply even/odd identities.
identities.nasa_application: Dot product of unit vectors: cos(angle between) = u·v. Central to orbital plane intersection calculations.

identities.name: Tangent of sum
identities.proof_hint: Divide sin(α+β) by cos(α+β), then divide numerator and denominator by cosα·cosβ.
identities.nasa_application: Adding small perturbation angles in guidance corrections. Feedback loop gain margin analysis.

identities.name: Tangent of difference
identities.proof_hint: Replace β with -β in the tangent sum formula and apply tan(-β) = -tanβ.
identities.nasa_application: Relative bearing calculations between two spacecraft or ground stations.

identities.name: Double angle (sine)
identities.proof_hint: Set α = β = θ in sin(α + β).
identities.nasa_application: Appears in the vis-viva equation derivatives and in computing orbital period from semi-major axis.

identities.name: Double angle (cosine, form 1)
identities.proof_hint: Set α = β = θ in cos(α + β).
identities.nasa_application: Spacecraft spin dynamics — nutation angle relates to moments of inertia through double-angle terms.

identities.name: Double angle (cosine, form 2)
identities.proof_hint: Replace sin²θ with 1 - cos²θ in form 1.
identities.nasa_application: Chebyshev polynomial recurrence: T_n(cosθ) = cos(nθ). Used in orbit propagation numerical methods.

identities.name: Double angle (cosine, form 3)
identities.proof_hint: Replace cos²θ with 1 - sin²θ in form 1.
identities.nasa_application: Haversine formula for great-circle distance: hav(θ) = sin²(θ/2) = (1 - cosθ)/2. GPS and ground track computation.

identities.name: Double angle (tangent)
identities.proof_hint: Set α = β = θ in tan(α + β).
identities.nasa_application: Control system analysis — doubling frequency response phase angles in Bode plots.

identities.name: Half angle (sine)
identities.proof_hint: Solve cos(2φ) = 1 - 2sin²φ for sinφ, then let φ = θ/2.
identities.nasa_application: Haversine navigation formula: hav(d/R) = hav(φ₂-φ₁) + cosφ₁·cosφ₂·hav(λ₂-λ₁). Ground station visibility.

identities.name: Half angle (cosine)
identities.proof_hint: Solve cos(2φ) = 2cos²φ - 1 for cosφ, then let φ = θ/2.
identities.nasa_application: Quaternion construction from rotation matrices — q_w = √(1 + trace(R))/2 involves this identity.

identities.name: Half angle (tangent)
identities.proof_hint: Divide sin(θ/2) by cos(θ/2), rationalize, and use double angle formulas.
identities.nasa_application: Universal variable formulation: the half-angle substitution t = tan(E/2) converts Kepler's equation to polynomial form (Battin's method).

identities.name: Product to sum (sinα·cosβ)
identities.proof_hint: Add the sum and difference formulas for sine.
identities.nasa_application: AM modulation analysis in deep space communication — carrier × signal produces sum and difference frequencies.

identities.name: Product to sum (cosα·cosβ)
identities.proof_hint: Add the sum and difference formulas for cosine.
identities.nasa_application: Lock-in amplifier signal extraction — multiplying signal by reference cosine and low-pass filtering.

identities.name: Product to sum (sinα·sinβ)
identities.proof_hint: Subtract the cosine sum formula from the cosine difference formula.
identities.nasa_application: Cross-spectral density computation in vibration analysis — identifying shared frequency content between sensors.

identities.name: Sum to product (sinα + sinβ)
identities.proof_hint: Let α = u + v and β = u - v, then apply the sine sum formula.
identities.nasa_application: Beat frequency detection in dual-frequency radar systems — sum of close frequencies produces amplitude modulation.

identities.name: Sum to product (sinα - sinβ)
identities.proof_hint: Let α = u + v and β = u - v, then subtract the sine difference formula.
identities.nasa_application: Differential Doppler measurement between two spacecraft for relative velocity determination.

identities.name: Sum to product (cosα + cosβ)
identities.proof_hint: Let α = u + v and β = u - v, then add the cosine sum and difference formulas.
identities.nasa_application: Superposition of electromagnetic waves in phased array antennas — constructive interference pattern.

identities.name: Sum to product (cosα - cosβ)
identities.proof_hint: Let α = u + v and β = u - v, then subtract the cosine sum from cosine difference formulas.
identities.nasa_application: Destructive interference nulls in antenna patterns — used to steer nulls away from interference sources.

identities.name: Power-reducing (sin²θ)
identities.proof_hint: Rearrange cos(2θ) = 1 - 2sin²θ.
identities.nasa_application: RMS power computation in AC circuits and signal processing — average of sin² over a full period equals 1/2.

identities.name: Power-reducing (cos²θ)
identities.proof_hint: Rearrange cos(2θ) = 2cos²θ - 1.
identities.nasa_application: Radiation pressure force calculations — intensity proportional to cos² of incidence angle on solar panels.

identities.name: Power-reducing (sin³θ)
identities.proof_hint: Write sin³θ = sinθ·sin²θ, apply power-reducing to sin²θ, then use product-to-sum.
identities.nasa_application: Third-harmonic distortion analysis in power amplifiers for deep space transmitters.

identities.name: Power-reducing (cos³θ)
identities.proof_hint: Write cos³θ = cosθ·cos²θ, apply power-reducing to cos²θ, then use product-to-sum.
identities.nasa_application: Nonlinear optics — third-order susceptibility terms in laser ranging systems (LAGEOS, LRO).

identities.name: Triple angle (sine)
identities.proof_hint: sin(3θ) = sin(2θ + θ). Expand using sum formula, then apply double angle formulas.
identities.nasa_application: Third-harmonic analysis in orbital perturbation theory — J₃ zonal harmonic of Earth's gravity field.

identities.name: Triple angle (cosine)
identities.proof_hint: cos(3θ) = cos(2θ + θ). Expand using sum formula, then apply double angle formulas.
identities.nasa_application: Chebyshev polynomial T₃(x) = 4x³ - 3x. Used in numerical orbit propagation and spectral methods.

identities.name: Triple angle (tangent)
identities.proof_hint: tan(3θ) = tan(2θ + θ). Apply tangent sum formula, then substitute tan(2θ).
identities.nasa_application: Higher-order phase margin analysis in multi-loop control systems.

identities.name: Law of Sines
identities.proof_hint: Drop altitude from vertex to opposite side. Express altitude two ways using sine of the angles. R is circumradius.
identities.nasa_application: Triangulation in deep space navigation — determining spacecraft position from angular measurements to known celestial objects.

identities.name: Law of Cosines
identities.proof_hint: Place triangle in coordinate system. Compute distance between two vertices using the distance formula.
identities.nasa_application: Range computation in orbit determination — given two position vectors and the angle between them, find the chord distance.

identities.name: Triangle area (sine form)
identities.proof_hint: Area = (1/2)·base·height. Height = a·sinC when base = b.
identities.nasa_application: Swept area in Kepler's second law: dA/dt = h/2 where h is specific angular momentum. Equal areas in equal times.

identities.name: Euler's formula
identities.proof_hint: Taylor series of e^(iθ) separates into real (cosine series) and imaginary (sine series) parts.
identities.nasa_application: Foundation of phasor analysis in all RF engineering. Signal processing, filter design, modulation theory for deep space communications.

identities.name: Euler's identity
identities.proof_hint: Set θ = π in Euler's formula: cos(π) = -1, sin(π) = 0.
identities.nasa_application: The most beautiful equation in mathematics. Connects the five fundamental constants: e, i, π, 1, 0. The unit circle at θ = π.

identities.name: Cosine from exponentials
identities.proof_hint: Add Euler's formula for θ and -θ, divide by 2.
identities.nasa_application: Digital filter design — cosine terms expressed as complex exponentials for Z-transform analysis in flight software.

identities.name: Sine from exponentials
identities.proof_hint: Subtract Euler's formula for -θ from θ, divide by 2i.
identities.nasa_application: Fourier transform kernel — every spectral analysis of telemetry data decomposes signals into these complex exponentials.

identities.name: De Moivre's theorem
identities.proof_hint: From Euler's formula: (e^(iθ))^n = e^(inθ). Expand both sides.
identities.nasa_application: Deriving multiple-angle formulas for harmonic analysis. Root-finding for orbital transfer polynomial equations.

identities.name: Arcsine negation
identities.proof_hint: Since sine is odd, if sinθ = x then sin(-θ) = -x.
identities.nasa_application: Symmetric declination calculations in celestial coordinate systems.

identities.name: Arccosine negation
identities.proof_hint: cos(π - θ) = -cosθ. If cosθ = x, then cos(π - θ) = -x.
identities.nasa_application: Supplementary angle relationships in orbit geometry — true anomaly measured from periapsis vs. apoapsis.

identities.name: Arctangent negation
identities.proof_hint: Since tangent is odd, if tanθ = x then tan(-θ) = -x.
identities.nasa_application: Symmetric phase response in control system Bode plots.

identities.name: Arcsine-arccosine complement
identities.proof_hint: If sinθ = x, then cos(π/2 - θ) = x, so arccos(x) = π/2 - θ.
identities.nasa_application: Converting between elevation angle and zenith distance in telescope pointing and star tracker software.

identities.name: Arctangent addition
identities.formula: arctan(a) + arctan(b) = arctan((a + b)/(1 - ab)) + kπ
identities.proof_hint: Apply tangent to both sides and use the tangent sum formula. k adjusts for quadrant.
identities.nasa_application: Combining bearing angles from multiple ground stations in spacecraft tracking.

identities.name: Two-argument arctangent (atan2)
identities.formula: atan2(y, x) = arctan(y/x) adjusted to [-π, π] by quadrant
identities.formula_latex: \text{atan2}(y, x) = \begin{cases} \arctan(y/x) & x > 0 \\ \arctan(y/x) + \pi & x < 0, y \geq 0 \\ \arctan(y/x) - \pi & x < 0, y < 0 \end{cases}
identities.proof_hint: The standard arctan loses quadrant information. atan2 preserves it using the signs of both x and y.
identities.nasa_application: THE critical function in aerospace software. Every attitude determination, every coordinate transformation, every guidance calculation. Avoids division by zero and quadrant ambiguity that arctan alone cannot resolve.

identities.name: Hyperbolic sine from circular sine
identities.nasa_application: Hyperbolic trajectories (escape orbits) — the hyperbolic anomaly H replaces eccentric anomaly E, and sinh(H) replaces sin(E) in Kepler's equation for e > 1.

identities.name: Hyperbolic cosine from circular cosine
identities.proof_hint: cos(ix) = (e^(i·ix) + e^(-i·ix))/2 = (e^(-x) + e^x)/2 = cosh(x).
identities.nasa_application: Catenary curves in tether dynamics (space elevator, tethered satellite systems). Gravity gradient stabilization analysis.

identities.name: Hyperbolic Pythagorean identity
identities.proof_hint: Direct computation: ((e^x + e^(-x))/2)² - ((e^x - e^(-x))/2)² = 1. Or: substitute ix into sin² + cos² = 1.
identities.nasa_application: Hyperbolic orbit geometry — the hyperbola x²/a² - y²/b² = 1 parameterized as x = a·cosh(t), y = b·sinh(t). Flyby trajectory shape.

identities.name: Hyperbolic exponential definitions
identities.proof_hint: These are the definitions. Compare with the Euler formula representations of sin and cos — the factor of i is what separates circular from hyperbolic.
identities.nasa_application: Thermal analysis — heat equation solutions in cylindrical spacecraft components use hyperbolic functions for radial temperature profiles.

identities.name: Hyperbolic tangent
identities.proof_hint: Direct from definitions of sinh and cosh.
identities.nasa_application: Relativistic velocity addition: v_combined = c·tanh(arctanh(v₁/c) + arctanh(v₂/c)). Also appears in neural network activation functions for onboard AI.

identities.name: Gudermannian function
identities.proof_hint: The Gudermannian connects circular and hyperbolic functions without complex numbers. It is the integral of sech(t).
identities.nasa_application: Mercator projection: latitude φ = gd(y/R). Every flat map of Earth used in mission planning connects circular (latitude) to hyperbolic (Mercator y-coordinate) through this function.

special_values.title: Exact Trigonometric Values at Standard Angles
special_values.note: These are the landmarks of the unit circle. Every engineer should know these cold.

domain_ranges.title: Domains and Ranges of Trigonometric and Inverse Trigonometric Functions
domain_ranges.note: The restricted domains for inverse functions are chosen to make them single-valued (one-to-one).
